steps.title: What this function answers
steps.narrative: Given a candidate replacement pGraph (an algebraic DAG over cut leaves) and a current root pRoot, return how many AIG ANDs would actually be added if we materialized the graph — taking strash-table reuse into account. This is the number every "is the move profitable?" check sits on top of: gain = MFFC size − count.

panes.title: Two pre-call setups
panes.body: (1) Cut leaves' pFunc fields are pre-set to the current AIG nodes that will play those roles in the materialized graph. (2) The MFFC of pRoot is already labeled with the current TravId (§0.4).

steps.title: Setup: assume rewrite chose cut {a, c, d, n6}
steps.narrative: From §3, rewrite picked the cut {a, c, d, n6} at f and labeled MFFC = {f, n8, n7, n9} with the current TravId. The candidate Dec_Graph has 3 internal ANDs over the 4 leaves.

panes.title: Candidate Dec_Graph_t (illustrative)
panes.body:
Three internal nodes m1, m2, m3 over leaves {a, c, d, n6}.
m1 = a · d     (reuses leaf a, d)
m2 = c · d     (reuses leaf c, d)
m3 = NOR(m1, m2, n6) — written in AIG as m3 = (~m1)·(~m2)·(~n6) wired into one AND-tree
The graph's root is m3 with negation, implementing n6 + (a·d) + (c·d) = ab + ad + cd.

steps.title: Iterate Dec_Graph nodes in order
steps.narrative:
Dec_GraphForEachNode walks each internal Dec_Graph node. For each:
  1. Look up its two child positions in the AIG via Abc_AigAndLookup(child0, child1).
  2. If the lookup hits AND the hit is NOT in the MFFC: reuse (no count).
  3. If lookup misses, OR hits but the result is in the MFFC: add (count += 1).
  4. Compute level. If level > LevelMax, return -1 (reject).

steps.title: Visit m1 = a · d → strash hits n7, but n7 ∈ MFFC → count
steps.narrative: Abc_AigAndLookup(a, d) finds the existing AND n7. But n7 has the current TravId (we labeled it during MFFC). It will be deleted along with the rest of the MFFC, so the lookup hit is illusory. Treat as add. count = 1.

steps.title: Visit m2 = c · d → hits n9, also in MFFC → count
steps.narrative: Same outcome: n9 exists but is MFFC-tagged. count = 2.

steps.title: Visit m3 = root → no existing AND with these children → count
steps.narrative:
m3 combines m1, m2, and n6 in a way that doesn't structurally match any existing AND in the strash. Abc_AigAndLookup misses → add. count = 3.

(In ABC's actual library, m3 is built as a small AND-tree of two binary ANDs internally — the "Cone = 3" in the rewrite trace counts those binary ANDs.)

steps.title: Return: nNodesAdded = 3 → gain = 4 − 3 = 1
steps.narrative: Final answer: 3 ANDs would be added if rewrite materializes this candidate. Combined with MFFC = 4, gain = 1 → accept (under default fUseZeros=0).

panes.title: When this function returns -1
panes.body: Two cases: (a) the running count exceeds NodeMax = MFFC size — bail out early (the move can't be profitable). (b) The new top-level node would equal pRoot structurally (a self-loop, decAbc.c:196..197) — also bail. Both are short-circuits; not all candidates need full evaluation.

steps.title: Why MFFC tagging matters
steps.narrative: Without the TravId check, rewrite would happily "reuse" n7 and n9 from the existing strash table, claim count = 1, gain = 3, and commit. But after committing, n7 and n9 — being inside the original MFFC — get deallocated by reference counting, leaving dangling pointers in the new graph. The TravId check at decAbc.c:202 is the safety belt: it forces the count to reflect what will actually exist post-commit.

panes.body: Same logic governs refactor (abcRefactor.c:207) and the various candidate-scoring sites in resub. All three operators commit only if Dec_GraphToNetworkCount's return strictly beats their nMinSaved threshold.
